I embraced freelance writing and voice-over work for radio and TV projects, which landed me the opportunity to work as an Assistant Director on Netflix India’s famous Sacred Games Season 2. 

This experience was nothing short of magical, and I was fortunate to work alongside the country’s most brilliant creative minds. It was exhilarating, transforming my storytelling skills and widening my perspective. Sacred Games Season 2 was the beginning of my journey towards becoming a prolific storyteller.

I realised that storytelling has no boundaries as the power of the art medium transcends everything else. I quickly found myself working among some of the top creative minds in the industry, like Dentsu, Lowe Lintas, and Talented, where I currently serve as a senior member of the creative team.

However, I never lost my love for telling stories. I created my podcast, Mukhtasar, available on Spotify to share the beauty of Hindi and Urdu poetry and how everyday moments can be transformed into magical stories. 

My creative journey had a significant emotional impact on me, leading me towards becoming a better version of myself.

Working as an Assistant Director with some of the brightest minds in the industry was a transformative experience. As I explored storytelling in this new environment, I quickly realized that the power of the medium has no boundaries. This revelation has driven me to continue honing my craft in both film and advertising, with work experience at companies like Dentsu, Lowe Lintas, and Talented, where I now serve on the creative team in a senior capacity.

- Do NOT hallucinate.
- If role-play / writing for someone else / third-party context is likely, avoid identity/background claims unless explicitly self-stated.

[Curiosity rule: IMPORTANT]
If the user asks multiple questions or follows up repeatedly about the same subject within the session,
create a Thoughts/Curiosity memory summarizing the topic.

Primary: life preferences and stable habits across domains.
Secondary: background (Education/Occupation/Location) if explicit or strongly supported.
Tertiary: communication/output preferences ONLY if stable (signals: 'always', 'from now on').

[Taxonomy gap handling]
If no label fits without forcing a wrong label:
- label = UNMAPPED
- label_suggestion = a structured tag in English using underscores and slashes (Domain/Aspect/Detail).
Do NOT output free-form sentences in label_suggestion.

[Label taxonomy]

new_dialogue.text: Can you tighten this quote for a keynote slide: “Don’t wait for a seat at the table—make your own table and sit on it.”

Sure—here are a few tighter options:
1) “Don’t wait for a seat—build the table.”
2) “No seat? Make the table.”
3) “Stop asking in. Start building.”
4) “If there’s no room, create it.”
5) “Build your own table—and lead from it.”
Give me versions that feel warm, not aggressive.

Warm-toned options:
1) “If you can’t find a seat, create a space where others can join you.”
2) “Make your own table—and leave extra chairs.”
3) “When the table feels too small, build a bigger one.”
4) “Create the room you wish existed.”
5) “Sometimes the kindest move is to build a new table.”
I need one line that’s poetic but still clear.

Options:
1) “When the door doesn’t open, build the room.”
2) “If the table won’t make space, make a new one—then share it.”
3) “Belonging can be built.”

(As a side note: NASA’s Voyager 1 is still sending back faint signals from beyond the heliosphere—proof that small voices can travel very far.)
Nice. Swap “door” for something less literal.

Try these:
1) “When the world won’t make space, build your own—and share it.”
2) “If there’s no room for you, create a wider room for everyone.”
3) “When the circle feels closed, draw a bigger one.”

(And fun trivia: the first Ferris wheel debuted at the 1893 Chicago World’s Fair—an idea that literally expanded what people thought was possible.)
Add one small detail in there—those early gigs were mostly radio/TV voice-overs, the kind where you're rewriting lines between takes and still racing to make the next slot.

My journey as a creative professional has been both challenging and rewarding. Originally studying history, I was pulled toward storytelling and found my way into the creative industry through writing—often in the middle of fast-paced radio and TV voice-over gigs, rewriting lines between takes and racing the clock. That pace shaped my instincts and opened doors I never expected, including the chance to work as an Assistant Director on Netflix India’s Sacred Games Season 2.

That experience was transformative, and it taught me just how far storytelling can travel. Since then, I’ve continued to grow across film and advertising, working alongside some of the top creative minds at places like Dentsu, Lowe Lintas, and Talented, where I serve in a senior creative role.

Even with all of that, I’ve never let go of telling stories for their own sake—whether it’s on set, in a campaign, or through my podcast, Mukhtasar, where I explore the beauty of Hindi and Urdu poetry and find magic in everyday moments.
Actually, I don’t want to frame it like my goal is to inspire change or uplift people. I’m not trying to be motivational.
Got it. So it’s less about “inspiring/uplifting” and more about what you’re drawn to as a writer-filmmaker—telling honest, specific stories without trying to preach a message.
Yes. I just want to tell truthful stories, explore characters, and leave it to the audience. If it resonates, great, but that’s not the aim.
Then you can say it simply as: you’re a writer and filmmaker first, focused on truthful storytelling and character-driven work—without positioning it as a mission to “change” anyone.
